title: Chapter 4: One Word
content: Day three. Or maybe four. Time got slippery without a schedule.

I'd established a routine, which was insane—establishing a routine during the apocalypse—but my gamer brain craved structure, and Luna seemed to thrive on it. Every morning: feed her by hand, receive system rewards. Every afternoon: check the peephole, assess the hallway situation, debate whether to venture further. Every evening: sit with her on the couch in the fading light and talk.
I did all the talking. Luna did all the listening.

She was changing. Not fast—nothing about her recovery was fast—but visibly, undeniably changing. Her skin had warmed from corpse-cold to room temperature. The blue tint around her lips had faded to a pale, almost-living pink. Her movements were more coordinated: she could stand and walk without that telltale zombie drift, could pick up objects with something approaching dexterity, could turn her head toward sounds with purpose instead of reflex.
But the biggest change was her eyes.

Those pupil-blown discs had contracted. Not to normal—never quite to normal—but enough that I could see a ring of dark brown iris around the black. And they tracked me. Not like a predator tracking prey. Like a person tracking someone they wanted to understand.

"You know what kills me?" I said, cracking open a can of chicken soup I'd heated with the last of my camping stove fuel. "I never learned your name. The system calls you Luna, but that's not—you had a name before. A real name. A life."

She was sitting beside me on the couch, her hands folded in her lap. Still cold, but less so. I'd given her one of my hoodies—a big gray one that hung off her shoulders—and she looked almost normal in it. Almost human.

"Did you work around here? You were in the hallway of Floor 12, so you must have lived on this floor. 12D, maybe? The guy in 12D was—I never saw him. Kept weird hours." I took a sip of soup. "Or maybe you were visiting someone. A boyfriend? Girlfriend? I'm not assuming."
Her lips moved.
I'd gotten better at reading her silent speech, but she was trying harder now—forming words deliberately instead of instinctively. Her brow furrowed with effort.
She stopped. Frustrated. Her jaw clenched, and a sound came out—a grunt of something that was almost anger. She tried again.
*Lu—*
"Luna?" I offered. "That what you're trying for? The system already—"
*No.*
One word. Clear. Rough as sandpaper, barely louder than a breath, but unmistakably a word. Human speech, formed by human vocal cords, pushed through a throat that hadn't spoken in days.
My soup can slipped. Chicken broth splashed on my jeans.
"Say it again," I breathed.

Luna's eyes met mine. There was something in them I hadn't seen before—*fear*. Not the mindless animal fear of a cornered creature, but the specific, articulate fear of someone who might fail at something that matters.
*No.*
"*No*," I repeated, my voice cracking. "You're saying that's not your name? Luna isn't your name?"
A tiny shake of her head.
"Then what is? Take your time. I'm not going anywhere."
She closed her eyes. Her hands came up to her throat, pressing against her vocal cords like she could force them to work through sheer will. The muscles in her neck stood out with effort.
A pause. A shuddering breath—the first voluntary breath I'd seen her take.
*I don't—*
Another pause. Her eyes opened, and the fear was deeper now, because she'd just discovered something worse than not being able to speak.
*I don't remember.*
The words came out in a whisper, broken and raw, but she'd spoken a complete sentence. Four words. Grammar. Meaning. Communication.
Luna could talk.
She just had nothing to say—because whatever the virus had taken from her body, it had taken her memories too.
"I'm sorry," I said. I didn't know what else to say. I reached for her hand, and she grabbed it with both of hers, gripping me like I was the only solid thing in a world made of smoke.

**ROMANCE SYSTEM — TARGET: LUNA**
**AFFECTION: 25%**
**DEVELOPMENT MILESTONE: First coherent speech**
**NOTE: Target's cognitive recovery is accelerating beyond baseline zombie parameters. This is not normal. Recommend monitoring.**
Not normal. The system itself was calling it not normal.

"It's okay not to remember," I told her, my thumb tracing circles on the back of her hand. "I'll remember for both of us. I'll remember that you like the beef more than the fruit. That you can't stand the smell of tuna. That you always sit on the left side of the couch because you like the view of the window."

Her grip loosened. Just a little. Just enough for her to lean against my shoulder, her hair falling across my arm in a black curtain, smelling of nothing now—the decay smell had faded over three days of feeding and care.
"Stay," she whispered.
"That's the second time you've said that."
"Stay," she said again, more firmly. Her first deliberate word, and she'd chosen it twice. *Stay.*
"I'm staying," I promised. "I'm right here."
**AFFECTION: 28%**

The number was climbing. Somewhere around 100%, the system would consider her romance quest "complete," and I'd get some massive reward—maybe more food, maybe weapons, maybe a way to survive this nightmare long-term. The gamer in me was counting, strategizing, optimizing.
But the part of me that had just heard a dead girl speak her first word in days—that part didn't care about the reward.
That part was just glad she could talk.
That night, I lay on my bed—Luna on the couch, wrapped in every blanket I owned—and stared at the ceiling while the watch glowed softly on my wrist.

**SYSTEM LEVEL: 1 — NOVICE**
**ROMANCES COMPLETED: 0/1**
**LUNA AFFECTION: 28%**
**NEXT LEVEL REQ: Complete 1 romance quest**
Seventy-two percent to go. At this rate—maybe three weeks? A month? Hard to say without knowing what bumped the number.

But something bothered me. The system's note about Luna's "abnormal" recovery. Zombies didn't talk. Zombies didn't remember, and they certainly didn't grieve the fact that they couldn't remember. Luna was doing both.
Which meant she wasn't just any zombie.
She was something else. Something the virus hadn't fully taken—something that feeding and care and, yes, love could bring back.
And if that was true, then the terrifying question wasn't how to survive the apocalypse.
It was how many others were like her.
The watch buzzed once—soft, almost gentle—and I could have sworn the screen flickered with a message I wasn't ready to read.

**SIGNAL DETECTED — Floor 8**
**NEW ROMANCE TARGET IN PROXIMITY**
**QUEST AVAILABLE**
I pulled the blanket over my head and told myself I'd deal with it in the morning.
The watch kept glowing.
